addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 # Nyuzi Processor 2 3 [![CI](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/workflows/CI/badge.svg)](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/actions?query=workflow%3ACI) 4 [![Codacy Badge](https://api.codacy.com/project/badge/Grade/fbafdd72749e459d8de6f381abc7436d)](https://www.codacy.com/app/jbush001/NyuziProcessor?utm_source=github.com&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=jbush001/NyuziProcessor&utm_campaign=Badge_Grade) 5 6 Nyuzi is an experimental GPGPU processor focused on compute intensive tasks. 7 It includes a synthesizable hardware design written in System Verilog, an 8 instruction set emulator, an LLVM based C/C++ compiler, software 9 libraries, and tests. It can be used to experiment with microarchitectural 10 and instruction set design tradeoffs. 11 12 **Documentation:**
13 14 The following instructions explain how to set up the Nyuzi development 15 environment. This includes an emulator and cycle-accurate hardware simulator, 16 which allow hardware and software development without an FPGA, as well as 17 scripts and components to run on FPGA. 18 19 ## Install Prerequisites 20 21 ### Linux (Ubuntu) 22 23 This requires Ubuntu 16 (Xenial Xeres) or later to get the proper package 24 versions. It should work for other distributions, but you will probably need 25 to change some package names. From a terminal, execute the following: 26 27 sudo apt-get -y install autoconf cmake make ninja gcc g++ bison flex python \ 28 python3 perl emacs openjdk-8-jdk swig zlib1g-dev python-dev \ 29 libxml2-dev libedit-dev libncurses5-dev libsdl2-dev gtkwave python3-pip 30 pip3 install pillow 31 32 **Note: Recent versions of cmake break building the LLVM toolchain. This can be worked 33 around by switching back to an older version of cmake: 34 ** 35 36 *Emacs is used for [verilog-mode](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilog-mode) AUTO macros. 37 The makefile executes this operation in batch mode* 38 39 ### MacOS 40 41 These instruction assume OSX Mavericks or later. 42 43 Install XCode from the Mac AppStore ([Click Here](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/xcode/id497799835?mt=12)). 44 Then install the command line compiler tools by opening Terminal and typing the 45 following: 46 47 xcode-select --install 48 49 Install python libraries: 50 51 pip3 install pillow 52 53 Install Homebrew from , then use it to install the remaining packages 54 from the terminal (do *not* use sudo): 55 56 brew install cmake bison swig sdl2 emacs ninja 57 58 *Alternatively, you could use [MacPorts](https://www.macports.org/) if that is installed on your system, but you will need to change some of the package names* 59 60 You may optionally install [GTKWave](http://gtkwave.sourceforge.net/) for analyzing 61 waveform files. 62 63 ### Windows 64 65 I have not tested this on Windows. Many of the libraries are cross platform, so 66 it should be possible to port it. But the easiest route is probably to run 67 Linux under a virtual machine like [VirtualBox](https://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Downloads). 68 69 ## Build (Linux & MacOS) 70 71 The following script will download and install the 72 [Nyuzi toolchain](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziToolchain) and 73 [Verilator](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilator) Verilog simulator. 74 Although some Linux package managers have Verilator, they have old versions. 75 It will ask for your root password a few times to install stuff. 76 77 ./scripts/setup_tools.sh 78 79 Build everything else: 80 81 cmake . 82 make 83 84 Run tests: 85 86 make tests 87 88 *If you are on a Linux distribution that defaults to python3, you may run into build 89 problems with the compiler. In tools/NyuziToolchain/tools/CMakeLists.txt, comment 90 out the following line:* 91 92 add_llvm_external_project(lldb) 93 94 Occasionally a change will require a new version of the compiler. To rebuild: 95 96 git submodule update 97 cd tools/NyuziToolchain/build 98 make 99 sudo make install 100 101 ## Next Steps 102 103 Sample applications are available in [software/apps](software/apps). You can 104 run these in the emulator by switching to the build directory and using 105 the run_emulator script (some need 3rd party data files, details are in 106 the READMEs in those directories). 107 108 For example, this will render a 3D model in the emulator: 109 110 cd software/apps/sceneview 111 ./run_emulator 112 113 To run on FPGA, see instructions in hardware/fpga/de2-115/README.md 114

file.content:
# Nyuzi Processor [![CI](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/workflows/CI/badge.svg)](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/actions?query=workflow%3ACI) [![Codacy Badge](https://api.codacy.com/project/badge/Grade/fbafdd72749e459d8de6f381abc7436d)](https://www.codacy.com/app/jbush001/NyuziProcessor?utm_source=github.com&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=jbush001/NyuziProcessor&utm_campaign=Badge_Grade) Nyuzi is an experimental GPGPU processor focused on compute intensive tasks. It includes a synthesizable hardware design written in System Verilog, an instruction set emulator, an LLVM based C/C++ compiler, software libraries, and tests. It can be used to experiment with microarchitectural and instruction set design tradeoffs. **Documentation:**
The following instructions explain how to set up the Nyuzi development environment. This includes an emulator and cycle-accurate hardware simulator, which allow hardware and software development without an FPGA, as well as scripts and components to run on FPGA. ## Install Prerequisites ### Linux (Ubuntu) This requires Ubuntu 16 (Xenial Xeres) or later to get the proper package versions. It should work for other distributions, but you will probably need to change some package names. From a terminal, execute the following: sudo apt-get -y install autoconf cmake make ninja gcc g++ bison flex python \ python3 perl emacs openjdk-8-jdk swig zlib1g-dev python-dev \ libxml2-dev libedit-dev libncurses5-dev libsdl2-dev gtkwave python3-pip pip3 install pillow **Note: Recent versions of cmake break building the LLVM toolchain. This can be worked around by switching back to an older version of cmake: ** *Emacs is used for [verilog-mode](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilog-mode) AUTO macros. The makefile executes this operation in batch mode* ### MacOS These instruction assume OSX Mavericks or later. Install XCode from the Mac AppStore ([Click Here](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/xcode/id497799835?mt=12)). Then install the command line compiler tools by opening Terminal and typing the following: xcode-select --install Install python libraries: pip3 install pillow Install Homebrew from , then use it to install the remaining packages from the terminal (do *not* use sudo): brew install cmake bison swig sdl2 emacs ninja *Alternatively, you could use [MacPorts](https://www.macports.org/) if that is installed on your system, but you will need to change some of the package names* You may optionally install [GTKWave](http://gtkwave.sourceforge.net/) for analyzing waveform files. ### Windows I have not tested this on Windows. Many of the libraries are cross platform, so it should be possible to port it. But the easiest route is probably to run Linux under a virtual machine like [VirtualBox](https://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Downloads). ## Build (Linux & MacOS) The following script will download and install the [Nyuzi toolchain](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziToolchain) and [Verilator](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilator) Verilog simulator. Although some Linux package managers have Verilator, they have old versions. It will ask for your root password a few times to install stuff. ./scripts/setup_tools.sh Build everything else: cmake . make Run tests: make tests *If you are on a Linux distribution that defaults to python3, you may run into build problems with the compiler. In tools/NyuziToolchain/tools/CMakeLists.txt, comment out the following line:* add_llvm_external_project(lldb) Occasionally a change will require a new version of the compiler. To rebuild: git submodule update cd tools/NyuziToolchain/build make sudo make install ## Next Steps Sample applications are available in [software/apps](software/apps). You can run these in the emulator by switching to the build directory and using the run_emulator script (some need 3rd party data files, details are in the READMEs in those directories). For example, this will render a 3D model in the emulator: cd software/apps/sceneview ./run_emulator To run on FPGA, see instructions in hardware/fpga/de2-115/README.md

cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.4)

set(CMAKE_MODULE_PATH ${CMAKE_MODULE_PATH} "${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}/cmake/")

set(NYUZI_COMPILER_ROOT /usr/local/llvm-nyuzi/ CACHE PATH "Path to nyuzi compiler install directory.")
set(NYUZI_COMPILER_BIN ${NYUZI_COMPILER_ROOT}/bin)

add_subdirectory(tools)
add_subdirectory(software)
add_subdirectory(hardware)
add_subdirectory(tests)

content.content:
1	This directory contains the hardware implementation of the processor. There are
2	three directories:
3	
4	- core/
5	  The GPGPU. The top level module is 'nyuzi'. Configurable options (cache size,
6	  associativity, number of cores) are in core/config.sv
7	- fpga/
8	  Components of a quick and dirty system-on-chip test environment. These
9	  are not part of the Nyuzi core, but are here to allow testing on FPGA.
10	  Includes an SDRAM controller, VGA controller, AXI interconnect, and other
11	  peripherals like a serial port. (Documentation is
12	  [here](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/wiki/FPGA-Test-Environment)).
13	  The makefile for the DE2-115 board target is in fpga/de2-115.
14	- testbench/
15	  Support for simulation, including mock peripherals.
16	
17	This project uses Emacs [Verilog Mode](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilog-mode)
18	to automatically generate wire definitions and resets. If you have Emacs installed,
19	type 'make autos' from the command line to update the definitions in batch mode.
20	
21	When building for simulation, the preprocessor macro `SIMULATION` is defined.
22	This is used in the code to disable portions of code during synthesis. If you
23	are creating a simulation project for another toolchain, make sure this is
24	defined.
25	
26	This design uses parameterized memories (FIFOs and SRAM blocks) in the modules
27	core/sram_1r1w.sv, core/sram_2r1w.sv, and core/sync_fifo.sv. By default, these
28	instantiate simulator versions, which are not synthesizable (at least not
29	efficiently).
30	
31	- For Altera parts, the build files define the preprocessor macro
32	  `VENDOR_ALTERA, which will use the megafunctions ALTSYNCRAM and SCFIFO.
33	- If you want to use this with a different vendor, create a `VENDOR_xxx define and
34	  add a new section that uses the appropriate module.
35	- For tools that generate memories using a separate memory compiler, running
36	  `make core/srams.inc` will generate an include file with all used memory
37	  sizes in the design. You can tweak the script tools/misc/extract_mems.py to
38	  change the module names or parameter formats.
39	
40	This project uses [Verilator](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilator) for
41	simulation by default. Typing make in this directory compiles an executable
42	'nyuzi_vsim' in the bin/ directory. It accepts the following command
43	line arguments (Verilog prefixes arguments with a plus sign):
44	
45	|          Argument               | Meaning        |
46	|---------------------------------|----------------|
47	| +bin=*hexfile*                  | Load this file into simulator memory at address 0. Each line contains a 32-bit little endian hex encoded value. |
48	| +trace                          | Print register and memory transfers to standard out.  The cosimulation tests use this to verify operation. |
49	| +statetrace                     | Write thread states each cycle into a file called 'statetrace.txt', read by visualizer app (tools/visualizer). |
50	| +memdumpfile=*filename*         | Write simulator memory to a binary file at the end of simulation. The next two parameters must also be specified for this to work |
51	| +memdumpbase=*baseaddress*      | Base address in memory to start dumping (hexadecimal) |
52	| +memdumplen=*length*            | Number of bytes of memory to dump (hexadecimal) |
53	| +autoflushl2                    | Copy dirty data in the L2 cache to system memory at the end of simulation before writing to file (used with +memdump...) |
54	| +profile=*filename*             | Periodically write the program counters to a file. Use with tools/misc/profile.py |
55	| +block=*filename*               | Read file into virtual block device, which it exposes as a virtual SD/MMC device.
56	| +dumpmems                       | Dump the sizes of all internal FIFOs and SRAMs to standard out and exit. Used by tools/misc/extract_mems.py |
57	| +jtag_port=*port*               | Opens a socket waiting for a connection on the given port. Commands received here will be sent over JTAG. See sim_jtag.sv for more details |
58	| +verilator+rand+reset+I         | Determine how memory/registers are initialized 0=all zeroes, 1 = all ones, 2 = random values (default 0) |
59	| +verilator+seed+N               | Set seed for random number generator; used to force deterministic behavior during debugging |
60	
61	The amount of RAM available in the testbench is hard coded to 16MB. To alter
62	it, change MEM_SIZE in testbench/verilator_tb.sv.
63	
64	The simulator exits when all threads halt by writing to the appropriate control
65	register.
66	
67	To write a waveform trace, set the cmake variable DUMP_WAVEFORM and rebuild.
68	From the build directory:
69	
70	   cmake -DDUMP_WAVEFORM=1 .
71	   make
72	
73	With this enabled, the simulator writes a file called `trace.vcd` in
74	"[value change dump](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value_change_dump)"
75	format in the current working directory. This can be with a waveform
76	viewer like [GTKWave](http://gtkwave.sourceforge.net/).
77	Waveform files become enormous quickly. Even running a minute of simulation
78	will produce gigabytes of trace data. A trick to reduce the size is to use
79	verilator pragmas to selectively disable tracing for modules. Working in
80	hardware/testbench/soc_tb.sv, start by disabling all tracing:
81	
82	    +/*verilator tracing_off*/
83	     module soc_tb(
84	
85	Then, selectively re-enable it for modules you are interested in by adding a
86	pragma above the module instantiation:
87	
88	    /*verilator tracing_on*/
89	    my_module module_i_want_to_trace(.*);
90	    /*verilator tracing_off*/
91	
92	The timescale is set to 1 ns by default, which simulates a 1 GHz clock speed.
93	
94	This has support for Verilator's ability to utilize multiple processors, which is
95	disabled by default. To enable, configure using the following and perform a clean
96	rebuild.
97	
98	    cmake -DENABLE_VERILATOR_THREADS=1 .
99	
100	### Support for VCS
101	
102	Template scripts have been added to support building and running with
103	
104	Synposys [VCS](https://www.synopsys.com/verification/simulation/vcs.html)
105	simulator. The VCS scripts are located in the scripts/ directory.
106	
107	vcsbuild.pl requires no arguments and builds the model. It will create an
108	executable named simv and two support directories csrc and simv.daidir all located
109	in the scripts/ directory. vcsbuild.pl uses TOP.sv in the hardware/testbench
110	directory as the testbench top. TOP.sv in the VCS build is analogous to
111	verilator_main.cpp in the verilator build.
112	
113	vcsrun.pl will run simulation. It accepts plus arguments in the same way as the
114	verilor_model. Any plus argument that is Verilog specific should work. It
115	supports +randomize=*\[1\|0\]* and +randseed=*seed*
116	
117	vcs.config in the build/ directory is used to configure the paths for VCS and Verdi
118	for both scripts (edit the file according to your site's installation).
119	
120	The Makefile in the hardware/ directory can be used to build the VCS model by
121	executing:
122	
123	    % make vcsbuild
124	
125	Waveform dumping can be enabled by executing:
126	
127	    % cmake -DDUMP_WAVEFORM=1 .
128	    % make clean; make vcsbuild
129	
130	If waveform dumping is enabled in VCS, the simulator writes a file called
131	`trace.fsdb` which can be opened with Verdi.
132	
133	Several apps can be found in the software/apps/ directory: doom, hello_world,
134	mandelbrot, plasma, quakeview, rotozoom, sceneview. The Makefile for doom,
135	hello_world, mandelbrot, quakeview, and sceneview support simulation with
136	Verilator by executing:
137	
138	    % ./run_verilator
139	
140	Similarly, the same apps support simulation with VCS by executing:
141	
142	    % ./run_vcs
143

file.content:
This directory contains the hardware implementation of the processor. There are
three directories:

- core/
  The GPGPU. The top level module is 'nyuzi'. Configurable options (cache size,
  associativity, number of cores) are in core/config.sv
- fpga/
  Components of a quick and dirty system-on-chip test environment. These
  are not part of the Nyuzi core, but are here to allow testing on FPGA.
  Includes an SDRAM controller, VGA controller, AXI interconnect, and other
  peripherals like a serial port. (Documentation is
  [here](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/wiki/FPGA-Test-Environment)).
  The makefile for the DE2-115 board target is in fpga/de2-115.
- testbench/
  Support for simulation, including mock peripherals.

This project uses Emacs [Verilog Mode](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilog-mode)
to automatically generate wire definitions and resets. If you have Emacs installed,
type 'make autos' from the command line to update the definitions in batch mode.

When building for simulation, the preprocessor macro `SIMULATION` is defined.
This is used in the code to disable portions of code during synthesis. If you
are creating a simulation project for another toolchain, make sure this is
defined.

This design uses parameterized memories (FIFOs and SRAM blocks) in the modules
core/sram_1r1w.sv, core/sram_2r1w.sv, and core/sync_fifo.sv. By default, these
instantiate simulator versions, which are not synthesizable (at least not
efficiently).

- For Altera parts, the build files define the preprocessor macro
  `VENDOR_ALTERA, which will use the megafunctions ALTSYNCRAM and SCFIFO.
- If you want to use this with a different vendor, create a `VENDOR_xxx define and
  add a new section that uses the appropriate module.
- For tools that generate memories using a separate memory compiler, running
  `make core/srams.inc` will generate an include file with all used memory
  sizes in the design. You can tweak the script tools/misc/extract_mems.py to
  change the module names or parameter formats.

This project uses [Verilator](http://www.veripool.org/wiki/verilator) for
simulation by default. Typing make in this directory compiles an executable
'nyuzi_vsim' in the bin/ directory. It accepts the following command
line arguments (Verilog prefixes arguments with a plus sign):

|          Argument               | Meaning        |
|---------------------------------|----------------|
| +bin=*hexfile*                  | Load this file into simulator memory at address 0. Each line contains a 32-bit little endian hex encoded value. |
| +trace                          | Print register and memory transfers to standard out.  The cosimulation tests use this to verify operation. |
| +statetrace                     | Write thread states each cycle into a file called 'statetrace.txt', read by visualizer app (tools/visualizer). |
| +memdumpfile=*filename*         | Write simulator memory to a binary file at the end of simulation. The next two parameters must also be specified for this to work |
| +memdumpbase=*baseaddress*      | Base address in memory to start dumping (hexadecimal) |
| +memdumplen=*length*            | Number of bytes of memory to dump (hexadecimal) |
| +autoflushl2                    | Copy dirty data in the L2 cache to system memory at the end of simulation before writing to file (used with +memdump...) |
| +profile=*filename*             | Periodically write the program counters to a file. Use with tools/misc/profile.py |
| +block=*filename*               | Read file into virtual block device, which it exposes as a virtual SD/MMC device.
| +dumpmems                       | Dump the sizes of all internal FIFOs and SRAMs to standard out and exit. Used by tools/misc/extract_mems.py |
| +jtag_port=*port*               | Opens a socket waiting for a connection on the given port. Commands received here will be sent over JTAG. See sim_jtag.sv for more details |
| +verilator+rand+reset+I         | Determine how memory/registers are initialized 0=all zeroes, 1 = all ones, 2 = random values (default 0) |
| +verilator+seed+N               | Set seed for random number generator; used to force deterministic behavior during debugging |

The amount of RAM available in the testbench is hard coded to 16MB. To alter
it, change MEM_SIZE in testbench/verilator_tb.sv.

The simulator exits when all threads halt by writing to the appropriate control
register.

To write a waveform trace, set the cmake variable DUMP_WAVEFORM and rebuild.
From the build directory:

   cmake -DDUMP_WAVEFORM=1 .
   make

With this enabled, the simulator writes a file called `trace.vcd` in
"[value change dump](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value_change_dump)"
format in the current working directory. This can be with a waveform
viewer like [GTKWave](http://gtkwave.sourceforge.net/).
Waveform files become enormous quickly. Even running a minute of simulation
will produce gigabytes of trace data. A trick to reduce the size is to use
verilator pragmas to selectively disable tracing for modules. Working in
hardware/testbench/soc_tb.sv, start by disabling all tracing:

    +/*verilator tracing_off*/
     module soc_tb(

Then, selectively re-enable it for modules you are interested in by adding a
pragma above the module instantiation:

    /*verilator tracing_on*/
    my_module module_i_want_to_trace(.*);
    /*verilator tracing_off*/

The timescale is set to 1 ns by default, which simulates a 1 GHz clock speed.

This has support for Verilator's ability to utilize multiple processors, which is
disabled by default. To enable, configure using the following and perform a clean
rebuild.

    cmake -DENABLE_VERILATOR_THREADS=1 .

### Support for VCS

Template scripts have been added to support building and running with

Synposys [VCS](https://www.synopsys.com/verification/simulation/vcs.html)
simulator. The VCS scripts are located in the scripts/ directory.

vcsbuild.pl requires no arguments and builds the model. It will create an
executable named simv and two support directories csrc and simv.daidir all located
in the scripts/ directory. vcsbuild.pl uses TOP.sv in the hardware/testbench
directory as the testbench top. TOP.sv in the VCS build is analogous to
verilator_main.cpp in the verilator build.

vcsrun.pl will run simulation. It accepts plus arguments in the same way as the
verilor_model. Any plus argument that is Verilog specific should work. It
supports +randomize=*\[1\|0\]* and +randseed=*seed*

vcs.config in the build/ directory is used to configure the paths for VCS and Verdi
for both scripts (edit the file according to your site's installation).

The Makefile in the hardware/ directory can be used to build the VCS model by
executing:

    % make vcsbuild

Waveform dumping can be enabled by executing:

    % cmake -DDUMP_WAVEFORM=1 .
    % make clean; make vcsbuild

If waveform dumping is enabled in VCS, the simulator writes a file called
`trace.fsdb` which can be opened with Verdi.

Several apps can be found in the software/apps/ directory: doom, hello_world,
mandelbrot, plasma, quakeview, rotozoom, sceneview. The Makefile for doom,
hello_world, mandelbrot, quakeview, and sceneview support simulation with
Verilator by executing:

    % ./run_verilator

Similarly, the same apps support simulation with VCS by executing:

    % ./run_vcs

content.content:
1	This is a Nyuzi instruction set emulator. It is not cycle accurate, and does not
2	simulate the behavior of the pipeline or caches, but is useful for several
3	purposes:
4	
5	- As a reference for co-verification.  When invoked in cosimulation mode
6	(`-m cosim`), it reads instruction side effects from the hardware model
7	via stdin. It steps its own threads and compare the results, flagging
8	an error if they do not match. More details are in tests/cosimulation/README.md
9	directory.
10	- For development of software.  This allows attaching a symbolic debugger
11	(described below).
12	- Performance modeling. This can generate memory reference traces and gather
13	statistics about instruction execution.
14	
15	This also simulates hardware peripherals supported by the FPGA and Verilog
16	simulator environments (which are all software compatible), such as video
17	output and a mass storage device.
18	
19	### Command line options
20	
21	|Option|Arguments                  |Meaning                                           |
22	|------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
23	| -v   |                           | Verbose, prints register transfers to stdout     |
24	| -m   |  mode                     | Mode is one of:                                  |
25	|      |                           | normal- Run to completion (default)              |
26	|      |                           | cosim- Cosimulation validation mode              |
27	|      |                           | gdb - Allow debugger connection on port 8000     |
28	| -f   |  widthxheight             | Display framebuffer output in window             |
29	| -d   |  filename,start,length    | Dump memory                                      |
30	| -b   |  filename                 | Load file into virtual block device              |
31	| -t   |  num                      | Threads per core (default 4)                     |
32	| -p   |  num                      | Number of cores (default 1)                      |
33	| -c   |  size                     | Total amount of memory                           |
34	| -r   |  instructions             | Screen refresh rate, number of instructions to execute between screen updates |
35	| -s   |  filename                 | Create the file and map emulated system memory onto it as a shared memory object |
36	| -i   |  filename                 | The passed filename is expected to be a named pipe. When bytes are sent over this pipe, it will emulate an external interrupt with the index in the byte. |
37	| -o   |  filename                 | The passed filename is expected to be a named pipe. Writing to the host interrupt register will send the 8-bit ID over the pipe. |
38	
39	The simulator assumes numeric arguments are decimals unless they are prefixed
40	with '0x', in which case it interprets them hexadecimal.
41	
42	Other notes:
43	
44	- Printfs from the emulated software will be written to the emulator standard
45	  out (via the virtual UART register)
46	- Memory starts at address 0. The emulator loads the memory image file (in the
47	  hexadecimal format that the Verilog $readmemh task uses) passed on the
48	  command line. It starts execution at address 0. The elf2hex utility, included
49	  with the toolchain, produces the hex file from an ELF file.
50	- The simulation exits when all threads halt (by writing to the appropriate
51	  control registers)
52	- Uncommenting the line `CFLAGS += -DLOG_INSTRUCTIONS=1` in the Makefile
53	  causes it to dump instruction statistics.
54	- See [SOC-Test-Environment](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/wiki/SOC-Test-Environment)
55	  for list of supported device registers. The emulator doesn't support the following devices:
56	
57	  - LED/HEX display output registers
58	  - Serial reads
59	  - VGA frame buffer address/toggle
60	  - SPI GPIO mode
61	
62	### Debugging with LLDB
63	
64	LLDB is a symbolic debugger built as part of the toolchain. Documentation
65	is available [here](http://lldb.llvm.org/tutorial.html). To use this,
66	you must compile the program with debug information enabled (the -g flag).
67	Many app makefiles have a 'debug' target that will start this automatically.
68	The steps to run the debugger manually are:
69	
70	1. Start emulator in GDB mode
71	
72	        bin/nyuzi_emulator -m gdb <program>.hex
73	
74	2. Start LLDB and attach to emulator. It should be in the directory that you
75	  built the program in, so it can find sources.
76	
77	        /usr/local/llvm-nyuzi/bin/lldb --arch nyuzi <program>.elf -o "gdb-remote 8000"
78	
79	Other notes:
80	
81	- The emulator does not support the debugger in cosimulation mode.
82	- Debugging works better if you compile the program with optimizations disabled.
83	  For example, at -O3, lldb cannot read variables if they are not live at the
84	  execution point.
85	- The debugger does not work with virtual memory enabled.
86	
87	It should be possible to use any GUI debugger that works with the GDB/MI
88	protocol, such as [Eclipse](https://eclipse.org/) or Emacs using the
89	lldb-mi executable that is installed with the toolchain, but I have not
90	tested this. There are some instructions
91	[here](https://www.codeplay.com/portal/lldb-mi-driver---part-2-setting-up-the-driver),
92	which would need to be adapted to this environment.
93	
94	### Tracing
95	
96	Another way of debugging is to enable verbose instruction logging. Change the
97	commandline to add the -v parameter:
98	
99	    bin/nyuzi_emulator -v program.hex
100	
101	This dumps every memory and register transfer to the console.
102	
103	Many test programs have a target to build the list file, but you can create
104	one like this:
105	
106	    /usr/local/llvm-nyuzi/bin/llvm-objdump -d -S program.elf > program.lst 2> /dev/null
107	
108	You can correlate the trace...
109	
110	    ```
111	    0000f43c [st 0] s0 <= 00010000
112	    0000f428 [st 1] s0 <= 00000001
113	    0000f414 [st 2] writeMemWord 000f7a74 00000000
114	    0000f400 [st 3] s0 <= 3e8a867a
115	    0000f440 [st 0] s30 <= 0000f444
116	    ```
117	
118	...with the listing to understand how the program is operating.
119	
120	    ```
121	    f428:    00 04 80 07                                      move s0, 1
122	    f42c:    1d 20 14 82                                      store_8 s0, 1288(sp)
123	    f430:    60 03 00 ac                                      getcr s27, 0
124	    f434:    5b 03 80 08                                      setne_i s26, s27, 0
125	    f438:    1a 02 00 f4                                      bnz s26, main+772
126	    f43c:    1f b0 ef a9                                      load_32 s0, -1044(pc)
127	    ```
128	
129	### Look up line numbers
130	
131	You can convert a program address can to a file/line combination with the
132	llvm-symbolizer program. This is not installed by default, but is in the
133	build directory for the toolchain:
134	
135	    echo <address> | tools/NyuziToolchain/build/bin/llvm-symbolizer -demangle -obj=<program>.elf
136

file.content:
This is a Nyuzi instruction set emulator. It is not cycle accurate, and does not
simulate the behavior of the pipeline or caches, but is useful for several
purposes:

- As a reference for co-verification.  When invoked in cosimulation mode
(`-m cosim`), it reads instruction side effects from the hardware model
via stdin. It steps its own threads and compare the results, flagging
an error if they do not match. More details are in tests/cosimulation/README.md
directory.
- For development of software.  This allows attaching a symbolic debugger
(described below).
- Performance modeling. This can generate memory reference traces and gather
statistics about instruction execution.

This also simulates hardware peripherals supported by the FPGA and Verilog
simulator environments (which are all software compatible), such as video
output and a mass storage device.

### Command line options

|Option|Arguments                  |Meaning                                           |
|------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| -v   |                           | Verbose, prints register transfers to stdout     |
| -m   |  mode                     | Mode is one of:                                  |
|      |                           | normal- Run to completion (default)              |
|      |                           | cosim- Cosimulation validation mode              |
|      |                           | gdb - Allow debugger connection on port 8000     |
| -f   |  widthxheight             | Display framebuffer output in window             |
| -d   |  filename,start,length    | Dump memory                                      |
| -b   |  filename                 | Load file into virtual block device              |
| -t   |  num                      | Threads per core (default 4)                     |
| -p   |  num                      | Number of cores (default 1)                      |
| -c   |  size                     | Total amount of memory                           |
| -r   |  instructions             | Screen refresh rate, number of instructions to execute between screen updates |
| -s   |  filename                 | Create the file and map emulated system memory onto it as a shared memory object |
| -i   |  filename                 | The passed filename is expected to be a named pipe. When bytes are sent over this pipe, it will emulate an external interrupt with the index in the byte. |
| -o   |  filename                 | The passed filename is expected to be a named pipe. Writing to the host interrupt register will send the 8-bit ID over the pipe. |

The simulator assumes numeric arguments are decimals unless they are prefixed
with '0x', in which case it interprets them hexadecimal.

Other notes:

- Printfs from the emulated software will be written to the emulator standard
  out (via the virtual UART register)
- Memory starts at address 0. The emulator loads the memory image file (in the
  hexadecimal format that the Verilog $readmemh task uses) passed on the
  command line. It starts execution at address 0. The elf2hex utility, included
  with the toolchain, produces the hex file from an ELF file.
- The simulation exits when all threads halt (by writing to the appropriate
  control registers)
- Uncommenting the line `CFLAGS += -DLOG_INSTRUCTIONS=1` in the Makefile
  causes it to dump instruction statistics.
- See [SOC-Test-Environment](https://github.com/jbush001/NyuziProcessor/wiki/SOC-Test-Environment)
  for list of supported device registers. The emulator doesn't support the following devices:

  - LED/HEX display output registers
  - Serial reads
  - VGA frame buffer address/toggle
  - SPI GPIO mode

### Debugging with LLDB

LLDB is a symbolic debugger built as part of the toolchain. Documentation
is available [here](http://lldb.llvm.org/tutorial.html). To use this,
you must compile the program with debug information enabled (the -g flag).
Many app makefiles have a 'debug' target that will start this automatically.
The steps to run the debugger manually are:

1. Start emulator in GDB mode

        bin/nyuzi_emulator -m gdb <program>.hex

2. Start LLDB and attach to emulator. It should be in the directory that you
  built the program in, so it can find sources.

        /usr/local/llvm-nyuzi/bin/lldb --arch nyuzi <program>.elf -o "gdb-remote 8000"

Other notes:

- The emulator does not support the debugger in cosimulation mode.
- Debugging works better if you compile the program with optimizations disabled.
  For example, at -O3, lldb cannot read variables if they are not live at the
  execution point.
- The debugger does not work with virtual memory enabled.

It should be possible to use any GUI debugger that works with the GDB/MI
protocol, such as [Eclipse](https://eclipse.org/) or Emacs using the
lldb-mi executable that is installed with the toolchain, but I have not
tested this. There are some instructions
[here](https://www.codeplay.com/portal/lldb-mi-driver---part-2-setting-up-the-driver),
which would need to be adapted to this environment.

### Tracing

Another way of debugging is to enable verbose instruction logging. Change the
commandline to add the -v parameter:

    bin/nyuzi_emulator -v program.hex

This dumps every memory and register transfer to the console.

Many test programs have a target to build the list file, but you can create
one like this:

    /usr/local/llvm-nyuzi/bin/llvm-objdump -d -S program.elf > program.lst 2> /dev/null

You can correlate the trace...

    ```
    0000f43c [st 0] s0 <= 00010000
    0000f428 [st 1] s0 <= 00000001
    0000f414 [st 2] writeMemWord 000f7a74 00000000
    0000f400 [st 3] s0 <= 3e8a867a
    0000f440 [st 0] s30 <= 0000f444
    ```

...with the listing to understand how the program is operating.

    ```
    f428:    00 04 80 07                                      move s0, 1
    f42c:    1d 20 14 82                                      store_8 s0, 1288(sp)
    f430:    60 03 00 ac                                      getcr s27, 0
    f434:    5b 03 80 08                                      setne_i s26, s27, 0
    f438:    1a 02 00 f4                                      bnz s26, main+772
    f43c:    1f b0 ef a9                                      load_32 s0, -1044(pc)
    ```

### Look up line numbers

You can convert a program address can to a file/line combination with the
llvm-symbolizer program. This is not installed by default, but is in the
build directory for the toolchain:

    echo <address> | tools/NyuziToolchain/build/bin/llvm-symbolizer -demangle -obj=<program>.elf

content.content:
1	This directory contains software that runs on the Nyuzi processor. It includes
2	libraries, apps, and benchmarks.
3	
4	## Running Applications
5	
6	Each program directory contains the following scripts to run the program in
7	the corresponding environment. These do not attempt to rebuild the program
8	first.
9	
10	- **run_emulator**: Execute the program in the emulator. This will pop up a
11	  framebuffer window to display output if necessary.
12	- **run_verilator**: Execute the program in the Verilator verilog simulator, which
13	  is the default simulator that is installed by the install scripts. This
14	  does not support a framebuffer, so you may need to make modifications to the
15	  program to make it dump its output and stop after rendering one frame.
16	- **run_vcs**: Execute program in
17	  [VCS](https://www.synopsys.com/verification/simulation/vcs.html) Verilog
18	  simulator if you have it installed.
19	- **run_fpga**: Transfer it over the serial port to the FPGA board. Instructions
20	  for setting up the FPGA are in hardware/fpga/de2-115/README.
21	- **run_debug**: Execute the program in the emulator and attach to it with the
22	  debugger (lldb).
23

file.content:
This directory contains software that runs on the Nyuzi processor. It includes
libraries, apps, and benchmarks.

## Running Applications

Each program directory contains the following scripts to run the program in
the corresponding environment. These do not attempt to rebuild the program
first.

- **run_emulator**: Execute the program in the emulator. This will pop up a
  framebuffer window to display output if necessary.
- **run_verilator**: Execute the program in the Verilator verilog simulator, which
  is the default simulator that is installed by the install scripts. This
  does not support a framebuffer, so you may need to make modifications to the
  program to make it dump its output and stop after rendering one frame.
- **run_vcs**: Execute program in
  [VCS](https://www.synopsys.com/verification/simulation/vcs.html) Verilog
  simulator if you have it installed.
- **run_fpga**: Transfer it over the serial port to the FPGA board. Instructions
  for setting up the FPGA are in hardware/fpga/de2-115/README.
- **run_debug**: Execute the program in the emulator and attach to it with the
  debugger (lldb).

content.content:
1	This directory contains all tests for this project. This contains tests both
2	for the core itself, as well as for tools and software libraries.
3	
4	# Running Tests
5	
6	In most directories is a script called 'runtest.py'. Invoking this with no
7	arguments will run all tests in that directory:
8	
9	    ./runtest.py
10	
11	To run a specific test, invoke this with the name of the test(s):
12	
13	    ./runtest.py jtag_idcode jtag_bypass
14	
15	By default, tests will be run on 'verilator' and 'emulator' targets if they
16	are supported by the test. This can be restricted to a specific target with the
17	--target flag:
18	
19	    ./runtest.py --target emulator aes.c
20	
21	You can list all available tests and targets with the list parameter:
22	
23	    ./runtest.py --list
24	
25	The --debug flag will enable printing test specific diagnostic output to the
26	console.
27	
28	There is an experimental 'fpga' target in progress, but is not fully functional
29	(See issue #122).
30	
31	Temporary files like assembled binaries are stored in the 'tests/work' folder.
32	These can be useful to dump disassembly and symbols, or to run directly in
33	the emulator or verilator to trace output. More tips on debugging are in the
34	README in tools/emulator.
35	
36	Invoking the top level Makefile with the test target will run tests in subprojects.
37	This is executed by the continuous integration environment.
38	
39	   make test
40	
41	The Makefile does not run the following tests:
42	
43	| Test Name            | Reason       |
44	|----------------------|--------------|
45	| stress/mmu/          | Takes a while to run, skipped to keep continuous integration tests quick.
46	| tools/lldb/          | LLDB is not installed in the CI environment's container. |
47	| core/multicore/      | Requires modifying hardware model to have 8 cores and rebuilding.
48	| csmith/              | Running on a 64-bit host produces different checksums than the program running on Nyuzi because of differences in native word width. Can still be useful to detect compiler crashes. |
49	| fail/                | These test cases validate the test harness itself and are meant to produce failure results. |
50	| float/               | Floating point conformance tests. There are still bugs in the hardware implementation that need to be fixed, and there are tweaks required to the test vectors to find canonical values where the spec is lenient. |
51	| fpga/                | These only run in the FPGA environment and validate things specific to the dev board (the test target only assembles them) |
52	| kernel/              | Only run in emulator, because they take a long time, and hang under verilator (issue #119) |
53	| kernel/multiprocess/ | These do not have automated harnesses and are not easy to
54	| kernel/threads/      |   automate for a number of reasons.
55	| kernel/vga/          |
56	| render/              | These only run under the emulator (they support verilator, but they take a long time to run)
57	
58	# Adding Tests
59	
60	Each test consists of a function, which takes two arguments: the name parameter (which
61	is useful to allow a function to support multiple tests), and a string target name which
62	controls how to execute the test (for example, on verilator or the emulator).
63	There are a few ways to add a new test. The easiest is to uses the 'test' decorator:
64	
65	    @test_harness.test
66	    def frobulate(name, target):
67	
68	This can include a parameter of which environments the test supports (by default, it will
69	support all):
70	
71	    @test_harness.test(['emulator'])
72	
73	Tests can also be added manually with several convenience functions:
74	
75	    test_harness.register_tests(my_test_func, ['my_test'], ['emulator'])
76	
77	runtests.py should not run any tests directly when the script is invoked, but
78	only register them. At the bottom of each runtests.py, call into test_harness
79	to invoke all tests that are enabled:
80	
81	    test_harness.execute_tests()
82	
83	If the test fails, it should throw a TestException, with a useful description:
84	
85	    if response != str.encode(value):
86	        raise test_harness.TestException(
87	            'unexpected response. Wanted ' + value + ' got ' + str(response))
88	
89	There are a number of built-in functions to check output:
90	
91	    def check_result(source_file, program_output):
92	
93	This will open 'source_file' and scan through it looking for the patterns CHECK and CHECKN.
94	These will verify that the output string following it either occurs in program_output or
95	does not, respectively. Check patterns can be embedded in comments. This is usually used
96	on the output of the program. For example:
97	
98	    printf("s1a 0x%08x\n", s1.a); // CHECK: s1a 0x12345678
99	
100	If you'd like to add additional output to a test to debug failures or the
101	test configuration, check the  test_harness.DEBUG flag, which will be set to true
102	if the user adds --debug to the command line.
103	
104	# Test Approach
105	
106	The validation strategy for the hardware implementation is to use a variety
107	of tests types to exercise it at different levels. Tests fall into the following
108	categories:
109	
110	1. **Module level hardware unit/integration tests** (tests/unit)
111	
112	   These test individual verilog modules, or combinations of them. These are
113	   not intended to be comprehensive (which would make them brittle).
114	   Because they have visibility into internal signals, and cycle precise
115	   timing control, they are useful for testing cases that are difficult to verify
116	   at the software level, for example, that flushing a cache line clears its
117	   dirty bit so it won't write it again.
118	
119	2. **System level directed functional tests** (tests/core)
120	
121	   These are intended to be comprehensive, covering all major instruction forms,
122	   operations, exception types, etc. They are self checking and report a failure
123	   or success message. These don't stress the system or catch race conditions
124	   (most are single threaded), but validate functional correctness. These run both
125	   in Verilog simulation and FPGA.
126	
127	3. **Constrained random cosimulation** (tests/cosimulation)
128	
129	   A test script generates random assembly programs. These are constrained,
130	   both to produce valid programs that don't crash immediately and have better
131	   coverage. Every instruction side effect is checked against the emulator model.
132	   These are multithreaded and stress the design. They are useful for finding
133	   race conditions and other hazards. But, since the emulator is not cycle
134	   accurate, this can't validate timing sensitive use cases like synchronized
135	   load/stores. These can only run in Verilog simulation. There is more detail
136	   in the README in the cosimulation directory.
137	
138	4. **Synthetic stress tests** (tests/stress)
139	
140	   Complementary to the random tests, these validate operations that the former
141	   cannot easily like atomic accesses or MMU operation. They often use an internal
142	   pseudorandom number generator to control their operation. Checking is done
143	   after the test as run, usually via a memory dump. These can run in Verilog
144	   simulation or on FPGA.
145	
146	5. **Whole program tests** (tests/whole-program, tests/kernel, tests/render)
147	
148	   These are real-world programs that do something useful like compute a
149	   cryptographic hash. The test harness verifies them by comparing their
150	   output (printed to the console) to expected values. These include
151	   everything from simple, single threaded programs to a full fledged kernel.
152	   Most can run both in Verilog simulation and on FPGA.
153

file.content:
This directory contains all tests for this project. This contains tests both
for the core itself, as well as for tools and software libraries.

# Running Tests

In most directories is a script called 'runtest.py'. Invoking this with no
arguments will run all tests in that directory:

    ./runtest.py

To run a specific test, invoke this with the name of the test(s):

    ./runtest.py jtag_idcode jtag_bypass

By default, tests will be run on 'verilator' and 'emulator' targets if they
are supported by the test. This can be restricted to a specific target with the
--target flag:

    ./runtest.py --target emulator aes.c

You can list all available tests and targets with the list parameter:

    ./runtest.py --list

The --debug flag will enable printing test specific diagnostic output to the
console.

There is an experimental 'fpga' target in progress, but is not fully functional
(See issue #122).

Temporary files like assembled binaries are stored in the 'tests/work' folder.
These can be useful to dump disassembly and symbols, or to run directly in
the emulator or verilator to trace output. More tips on debugging are in the
README in tools/emulator.

Invoking the top level Makefile with the test target will run tests in subprojects.
This is executed by the continuous integration environment.

   make test

The Makefile does not run the following tests:

| Test Name            | Reason       |
|----------------------|--------------|
| stress/mmu/          | Takes a while to run, skipped to keep continuous integration tests quick.
| tools/lldb/          | LLDB is not installed in the CI environment's container. |
| core/multicore/      | Requires modifying hardware model to have 8 cores and rebuilding.
| csmith/              | Running on a 64-bit host produces different checksums than the program running on Nyuzi because of differences in native word width. Can still be useful to detect compiler crashes. |
| fail/                | These test cases validate the test harness itself and are meant to produce failure results. |
| float/               | Floating point conformance tests. There are still bugs in the hardware implementation that need to be fixed, and there are tweaks required to the test vectors to find canonical values where the spec is lenient. |
| fpga/                | These only run in the FPGA environment and validate things specific to the dev board (the test target only assembles them) |
| kernel/              | Only run in emulator, because they take a long time, and hang under verilator (issue #119) |
| kernel/multiprocess/ | These do not have automated harnesses and are not easy to
| kernel/threads/      |   automate for a number of reasons.
| kernel/vga/          |
| render/              | These only run under the emulator (they support verilator, but they take a long time to run)

# Adding Tests

Each test consists of a function, which takes two arguments: the name parameter (which
is useful to allow a function to support multiple tests), and a string target name which
controls how to execute the test (for example, on verilator or the emulator).
There are a few ways to add a new test. The easiest is to uses the 'test' decorator:

    @test_harness.test
    def frobulate(name, target):

This can include a parameter of which environments the test supports (by default, it will
support all):

    @test_harness.test(['emulator'])

Tests can also be added manually with several convenience functions:

    test_harness.register_tests(my_test_func, ['my_test'], ['emulator'])

runtests.py should not run any tests directly when the script is invoked, but
only register them. At the bottom of each runtests.py, call into test_harness
to invoke all tests that are enabled:

    test_harness.execute_tests()

If the test fails, it should throw a TestException, with a useful description:

    if response != str.encode(value):
        raise test_harness.TestException(
            'unexpected response. Wanted ' + value + ' got ' + str(response))

There are a number of built-in functions to check output:

    def check_result(source_file, program_output):

This will open 'source_file' and scan through it looking for the patterns CHECK and CHECKN.
These will verify that the output string following it either occurs in program_output or
does not, respectively. Check patterns can be embedded in comments. This is usually used
on the output of the program. For example:

    printf("s1a 0x%08x\n", s1.a); // CHECK: s1a 0x12345678

If you'd like to add additional output to a test to debug failures or the
test configuration, check the  test_harness.DEBUG flag, which will be set to true
if the user adds --debug to the command line.

# Test Approach

The validation strategy for the hardware implementation is to use a variety
of tests types to exercise it at different levels. Tests fall into the following
categories:

1. **Module level hardware unit/integration tests** (tests/unit)

   These test individual verilog modules, or combinations of them. These are
   not intended to be comprehensive (which would make them brittle).
   Because they have visibility into internal signals, and cycle precise
   timing control, they are useful for testing cases that are difficult to verify
   at the software level, for example, that flushing a cache line clears its
   dirty bit so it won't write it again.

2. **System level directed functional tests** (tests/core)

   These are intended to be comprehensive, covering all major instruction forms,
   operations, exception types, etc. They are self checking and report a failure
   or success message. These don't stress the system or catch race conditions
   (most are single threaded), but validate functional correctness. These run both
   in Verilog simulation and FPGA.

3. **Constrained random cosimulation** (tests/cosimulation)

   A test script generates random assembly programs. These are constrained,
   both to produce valid programs that don't crash immediately and have better
   coverage. Every instruction side effect is checked against the emulator model.
   These are multithreaded and stress the design. They are useful for finding
   race conditions and other hazards. But, since the emulator is not cycle
   accurate, this can't validate timing sensitive use cases like synchronized
   load/stores. These can only run in Verilog simulation. There is more detail
   in the README in the cosimulation directory.

4. **Synthetic stress tests** (tests/stress)

   Complementary to the random tests, these validate operations that the former
   cannot easily like atomic accesses or MMU operation. They often use an internal
   pseudorandom number generator to control their operation. Checking is done
   after the test as run, usually via a memory dump. These can run in Verilog
   simulation or on FPGA.

5. **Whole program tests** (tests/whole-program, tests/kernel, tests/render)

   These are real-world programs that do something useful like compute a
   cryptographic hash. The test harness verifies them by comparing their
   output (printed to the console) to expected values. These include
   everything from simple, single threaded programs to a full fledged kernel.
   Most can run both in Verilog simulation and on FPGA.

16	
17	`ifndef __CONFIG_V
18	`define __CONFIG_V
19	
20	//
21	// Configurable parameters
22	// - THREADS_PER_CORE must be 2 or greater.
23	// - Number of cache ways must be 1, 2, 4, or 8 (TLB_WAYS does not have
24	//   this constraint). This is a limitation in the cache_lru module.
25	// - L1D_WAYS/L1I_WAYS must be greater than or equal to THREADS_PER_CORE,
26	//   otherwise the system may livelock on a cache miss.
27	// - The number of cache sets must be a power of two.
28	// - If you change the number of L2 ways, you must also modify the
29	//   flush_l2_cache function in testbench/soc_tb.sv. Comments above
30	//   that function describe how and why.
31	// - NUM_CORES must be 1-16. To synthesize more cores, increase the
32	//   width of core_id_t in defines.sv (as above, comments there describe why).
33	// - L1D_SETS sets must be 64 or fewer (page size / cache line size). This
34	//   avoids aliasing in the virtually indexed/physically tagged L1 cache by
35	//   preventing the same physical address from appearing in different cache
36	//   sets (see dcache_tag_stage).
37	// - The size of a cache is sets * ways * cache line size (64 bytes)
38	//
39	
40	`define NUM_CORES 1
41	`define THREADS_PER_CORE 4
42	`define L1D_WAYS 4
43	`define L1D_SETS 64        // 16k
44	`define L1I_WAYS 4
45	`define L1I_SETS 64        // 16k
46	`define L2_WAYS 8
47	`define L2_SETS 256        // 128k
48	`define AXI_DATA_WIDTH 32
49	`define ITLB_ENTRIES 64
50	`define DTLB_ENTRIES 64
51	`define TLB_WAYS 4
52	
53	// Picked random part version and number to have unique pattern to verify.
54	// The manufacturer ID is chosen to be the last possible ID.
55	`define JTAG_PART_VERSION 4
56	`define JTAG_PART_NUMBER 'hd20d
57	`define JTAG_MANUFACTURER_ID {4'b1111,  7'b1111101}
58	
59	`endif
60

1	//
2	// Copyright 2011-2015 Jeff Bush
3	//
4	// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
5	// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
6	// You may obtain a copy of the License at
7	//
8	//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

13	// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
14	// limitations under the License.

16	
17	`include "defines.svh"
18	
19	import defines::*;
20	
21	//
22	// Top level block for processor. Contains all cores and L2 cache, connects
23	// to AXI system bus.
24	//
25	
26	module nyuzi
27	    #(parameter RESET_PC = 0,
28	    parameter NUM_INTERRUPTS = 16)
29	
30	    (input                          clk,
31	    input                           reset,
32	    axi4_interface.master           axi_bus,
33	    io_bus_interface.master         io_bus,
34	    jtag_interface.target           jtag,
35	    input [NUM_INTERRUPTS - 1:0]    interrupt_req);
36	
37	    l2req_packet_t l2i_request[`NUM_CORES];
38	    logic[`NUM_CORES - 1:0] l2i_request_valid;
39	    ioreq_packet_t ior_request[`NUM_CORES];
40	    logic[`NUM_CORES - 1:0] ior_request_valid;
41	    logic[TOTAL_THREADS - 1:0] thread_en;
42	    scalar_t cr_data_to_host[`NUM_CORES];
43	    scalar_t data_to_host;
44	    logic[`NUM_CORES - 1:0] core_injected_complete;
45	    logic[`NUM_CORES - 1:0] core_injected_rollback;
46	    logic[`NUM_CORES - 1:0][TOTAL_THREADS - 1:0] core_suspend_thread;
47	    logic[`NUM_CORES - 1:0][TOTAL_THREADS - 1:0] core_resume_thread;
48	    logic[TOTAL_THREADS - 1:0] thread_suspend_mask;
49	    logic[TOTAL_THREADS - 1:0] thread_resume_mask;
50	
51	    /*AUTOLOGIC*/
52	    // Beginning of automatic wires (for undeclared instantiated-module outputs)
53	    logic               ii_ready [`NUM_CORES];  // From io_interconnect of io_interconnect.v
54	    iorsp_packet_t      ii_response;            // From io_interconnect of io_interconnect.v
55	    logic               ii_response_valid;      // From io_interconnect of io_interconnect.v
56	    logic               l2_ready [`NUM_CORES];  // From l2_cache of l2_cache.v
57	    l2rsp_packet_t      l2_response;            // From l2_cache of l2_cache.v
58	    logic               l2_response_valid;      // From l2_cache of l2_cache.v
59	    core_id_t           ocd_core;               // From on_chip_debugger of on_chip_debugger.v
60	    scalar_t            ocd_data_from_host;     // From on_chip_debugger of on_chip_debugger.v
61	    logic               ocd_data_update;        // From on_chip_debugger of on_chip_debugger.v
62	    logic               ocd_halt;               // From on_chip_debugger of on_chip_debugger.v
63	    logic               ocd_inject_en;          // From on_chip_debugger of on_chip_debugger.v
64	    scalar_t            ocd_inject_inst;        // From on_chip_debugger of on_chip_debugger.v
65	    local_thread_idx_t  ocd_thread;             // From on_chip_debugger of on_chip_debugger.v
66	    // End of automatics
67	
68	    initial
69	    begin
70	        // Check config (see config.svh for rules)
71	        assert(`NUM_CORES >= 1 && `NUM_CORES <= (1 << CORE_ID_WIDTH));
72	        assert(`L1D_WAYS >= `THREADS_PER_CORE);
73	        assert(`L1I_WAYS >= `THREADS_PER_CORE);
74	    end
75	
76	    // Thread enable
77	    always @*
78	    begin
79	        thread_suspend_mask = '0;
80	        thread_resume_mask = '0;
81	        for (int i = 0; i < `NUM_CORES; i++)
82	        begin
83	            thread_suspend_mask |= core_suspend_thread[i];
84	            thread_resume_mask |= core_resume_thread[i];
85	        end
86	    end
87	
88	    always_ff @(posedge clk, posedge reset)
89	    begin
90	        if (reset)
91	            thread_en <= 1;
92	        else
93	            thread_en <= (thread_en | thread_resume_mask) & ~thread_suspend_mask;
94	    end
95	
96	    l2_cache l2_cache(
97	        .l2_perf_events(),
98	        .*);
99	
100	    io_interconnect io_interconnect(.*);
101	
102	    on_chip_debugger on_chip_debugger(
103	        .jtag(jtag),
104	        .injected_complete(|core_injected_complete),
105	        .injected_rollback(|core_injected_rollback),
106	        .*);
107	
108	    generate
109	        if (`NUM_CORES > 1)
110	            assign data_to_host = cr_data_to_host[CORE_ID_WIDTH'(ocd_core)];
111	        else
112	            assign data_to_host = cr_data_to_host[0];
113	    endgenerate
114	
115	    genvar core_idx;
116	    generate
117	        for (core_idx = 0; core_idx < `NUM_CORES; core_idx++)
118	        begin : core_gen
119	            core #(
120	                .CORE_ID(core_id_t'(core_idx)),
121	                .NUM_INTERRUPTS(NUM_INTERRUPTS),
122	                .RESET_PC(RESET_PC)
123	            ) core(
124	                .l2i_request_valid(l2i_request_valid[core_idx]),
125	                .l2i_request(l2i_request[core_idx]),
126	                .l2_ready(l2_ready[core_idx]),
127	                .thread_en(thread_en[core_idx * `THREADS_PER_CORE+:`THREADS_PER_CORE]),
128	                .ior_request_valid(ior_request_valid[core_idx]),
129	                .ior_request(ior_request[core_idx]),
130	                .ii_ready(ii_ready[core_idx]),
131	                .ii_response(ii_response),
132	                .cr_data_to_host(cr_data_to_host[core_idx]),
133	                .injected_complete(core_injected_complete[core_idx]),
134	                .injected_rollback(core_injected_rollback[core_idx]),
135	                .cr_suspend_thread(core_suspend_thread[core_idx]),
136	                .cr_resume_thread(core_resume_thread[core_idx]),
137	                .*);
138	        end
139	    endgenerate
140	endmodule
141

21	//
22	// A single instruction pipeline with L1 instruction & data caches and L2
23	// interface logic.

25	
26	module core
27	    #(parameter CORE_ID = '0,
28	    parameter RESET_PC = '0,
29	    parameter NUM_INTERRUPTS = 16)
30	
31	    (input                                 clk,
32	    input                                  reset,

43	    output l2req_packet_t                  l2i_request,
44	
45	    // From io_request_queue
46	    input                                  ii_ready,
47	    input                                  ii_response_valid,
48	    input iorsp_packet_t                   ii_response,
49	
50	    // To io_request_queue
51	    output logic                           ior_request_valid,
52	    output ioreq_packet_t                  ior_request,
53	
54	    // From on_chip_debugger
55	    input                                  ocd_halt,
56	    input local_thread_idx_t               ocd_thread,
57	    input core_id_t                        ocd_core,
58	    input scalar_t                         ocd_inject_inst,
59	    input                                  ocd_inject_en,
60	    output logic                           injected_complete,
61	    output logic                           injected_rollback,
62	    input scalar_t                         ocd_data_from_host,
63	    input                                  ocd_data_update,
64	    output scalar_t                        cr_data_to_host,
65	
66	    // To nyuzi
67	    output logic[TOTAL_THREADS - 1:0]      cr_suspend_thread,
68	    output logic[TOTAL_THREADS - 1:0]      cr_resume_thread);
69	
70	    localparam EVENT_IDX_WIDTH = $clog2(CORE_PERF_EVENTS);
71	    localparam NUM_PERF_COUNTERS = 2;
72	
73	    logic core_selected_debug;
74	    logic[CORE_PERF_EVENTS - 1:0] perf_events;
75	    logic[NUM_PERF_COUNTERS - 1:0][EVENT_IDX_WIDTH - 1:0] perf_event_select;
76	
77	    /*AUTOLOGIC*/
78	    // Beginning of automatic wires (for undeclared instantiated-module outputs)
79	    scalar_t            cr_creg_read_val;       // From control_registers of control_registers.v
80	    logic [ASID_WIDTH-1:0] cr_current_asid [`THREADS_PER_CORE];// From control_registers of control_registers.v
81	    scalar_t            cr_eret_address [`THREADS_PER_CORE];// From control_registers of control_registers.v
82	    subcycle_t          cr_eret_subcycle [`THREADS_PER_CORE];// From control_registers of control_registers.v
83	    local_thread_bitmap_t cr_interrupt_en;      // From control_registers of control_registers.v
